title: New Media, One Year In | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: a16z details its one-year-old 'New Media' platform as a 'go-direct as a service' for portfolio founders, built on four motions (in-house creative, owned channels, concierge portco services, and a networked talent engine) and argues that in an attention-saturated world, winning capital and customers now hinges on being interesting, owning distribution, and compounding personal brand signal over time.

insights.claim: a16z has built a one-year-old internal function called 'New Media' that operates as 'go-direct as a service' for startups, structured around four motions: in-house creative, owned distribution channels, full-service concierge portfolio support, and a networked talent engine augmented by software and AI.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Signals that top-tier VC is industrializing founder marketing as a core platform service; founders and competing firms should treat go-direct media capability as a differentiating fund feature, not a nice-to-have.
insights.quote: at first estimate you can think of a16z New Media is “go-direct as a service” for startups. We have four primary motions
insights.locator: What do we actually do?

insights.claim: a16z uses a 'Reverse Pitch' (which competitors call 'the Bear Hug') in which the firm pitches the founder on the resources it will deploy and follows up with roughly 20 tangible commitments in the first week of working together, with New Media now a headline element of that pitch.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Shows the competitive mechanics of elite venture deal-winning; media/brand support is being weaponized to win allocation in hot rounds.
insights.quote: we’ll follow up the next morning with 20 tangible things we’ll help you with in our first week of working together.
insights.locator: If you’re ever pitching a16z

themes.theme: Old Media vs New Media regime shift

insights.claim: Media has shifted from a polished, defensive 'don't say anything that gets you in trouble' discipline dependent on gatekeeper channels into an authentic, unfiltered practice where being interesting is the decisive advantage.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Reframes corporate comms strategy: defensive PR loses to authenticity; investors should weight a founder's ability to be interesting as a real competitive moat.
insights.quote: to win you must go direct, and to go direct you must be interesting.
insights.locator: Ben & Marc are both genuine aficionados

insights.claim: The old binary that a founder was 'either journalism or PR flack' has collapsed, opening a new middle category where founders can tell their own story and be authentically understood without relying on a gatekeeping press.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: The decline of press gatekeeping power means traditional media businesses lose leverage while owned-channel and creator-led distribution capture the value.
insights.quote: the old line used to be, “Either you’re journalism, or you’re PR Flack.”
insights.locator: Even worse, the old line

insights.claim: New Media is not actually new content but rather a deliberate offensive business strategy and a type of packaging, now pursued by ambitious people and 'very profitable business models' because the world changed in ways that reward being interesting over everything else.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Points to investable business models built around attention/packaging; the edge is structural strategy, not novel media formats.
insights.quote: now there are ambitious people, and very profitable business models, pursuing New Media as a deliberate offensive business strategy.
insights.locator: The funny thing about New Media

themes.theme: Launch Videos and in-house creative speed

insights.claim: Launch Videos are framed as a new and enduring format letting founders make a statement at a critical moment without needing permission from another outlet, with a16z's in-house video team producing them on timelines agencies call impossible.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Speed-to-market in creative is being treated as alpha; agencies with multi-month lead times are structurally disadvantaged versus embedded VC creative teams.
insights.quote: When video production timelines stretch into months (which is usually the kind of lead time an agency asks for), you’re probably going to lose some of the magic.
insights.locator: “Tell your whole story

insights.claim: For the World Labs Marble launch, a16z's New Media team proposed shooting the announcement video on a 3D LED volume stage with the company's own product generating the environments in real time, and the launch went viral; per Dr. Fei-Fei Li, World Labs had not yet built its marketing muscle.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Concrete proof point that product-native creative can drive viral launches; a notable named case study for AI/spatial-computing portfolio companies.
insights.quote: They wanted to shoot our announcement video on a 3D LED volume stage, with our own product generating the environments in real time.
insights.locator: Here’s AI legend Dr. Fei-Fei Li

insights.claim: a16z claims it can hire better creative talent than the average agency because the job is more interesting and more incentive-aligned, with founders preferring to work with people like Gaby Goldberg, Alex Danco and James Reina who are 'on their level.'
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Talent and incentive alignment, not just budget, is the wedge VC uses to disintermediate marketing agencies.
insights.quote: we can hire much better people than your average agency. The job is more interesting, and the work is more incentive-aligned.
insights.locator: Now, this in-house creative work

insights.claim: a16z owns large distribution assets it can guarantee to founders: an X account with a million followers, a daily newsletter with a quarter million subscribers, a podcast with a million downloads a month, and ~160K and fast-growing Instagram followers.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Owned distribution at this scale is a durable, hard-to-replicate competitive asset for a fund; quantifies the reach moat new entrants must match.
insights.quote: We have our X account, with a million followers
insights.locator: Now, “New Media” as an open playing field

insights.claim: a16z explicitly disavows fake engagement, calling out competitors' launches with metrics 'that don't pass the smell test,' positioning authentic owned-channel reach as both a guarantee and a reputational safeguard.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Flags that engagement-metric inflation is widespread in startup launches; investors should discount headline launch numbers without authentic distribution behind them.
insights.quote: we’ve all seen launches with engagement metrics that don’t pass the smell test, if you know what we’re getting at.
insights.locator: There are two reasons why this is important.

insights.claim: Owning channels lets a16z avoid 'measuring out' founders' access and instead share distribution generously because 'no one can tell us no,' framing distribution generosity as helpful-maxxing rather than gatekeeping.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Channel ownership converts media leverage from a scarce favor into an abundant platform feature, deepening founder lock-in to the firm.
insights.quote: we’re about helpful-maxxing, not gatekeeping and withholding our distribution only for the very select.
insights.locator: Our goal with our channels

themes.theme: Signal, preferential attachment and Insider Media

insights.claim: Venture capital and startups are fundamentally an exercise in preferential attachment, where people must eagerly want to attach to a founder's idea, product and vision amid limitless competing opportunities.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: A mental model for evaluating startups: the ability to attract attachment (talent, customers, capital) is itself a measurable competitive variable.
insights.quote: venture capital, and startups in general, are an exercise in preferential attachment.
insights.locator: It’s important that founders are interesting!

insights.claim: A core New Media principle is that signal is what matters and people confer signal, not brands; founders, partners, researchers, power users and great X accounts are the characters who make an idea legible and credible.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Brand value is migrating from companies to individuals; investors should track founder/operator personal brand as a real asset that can move with people.
insights.quote: signal is what’s important, and people confer signal, not brands.
insights.locator: The second reason is more interesting

insights.claim: a16z endorses Anu's framing that 'New Media is Insider Media' (media for insiders by insiders where proximity is the product) but diverges by arguing being in-group is more positive-sum than people think because tech is a growing pie with more interesting people and ideas each year.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Counters the 'echo chamber' critique of insider tech media by reframing it as expandable; relevant to assessing the durability of creator/insider-media business models.
insights.quote: “being in-group” is more positive-sum than people think. The whole point of tech is that it’s a growing pie
insights.locator: Anu is right on with her definition

themes.theme: Concierge portco services and business impact

insights.claim: a16z's portfolio services team coordinates launches concierge-style by stitching together video assets, writing, press and timing, and still uses traditional comms because 'the right story in the right publication can still matter a lot,' working as part of a larger founder-framed system.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Traditional press is not dead but subordinated; the integrated system (owned + earned) is the unit of competitive advantage, not any single channel.
insights.quote: the press hit works best when it is part of the larger system, and undergirded by the founder’s own framing.
insights.locator: After the close, our “portco services” team

insights.claim: a16z claims its launches drive measurable business impact, including startups gaining tens of millions of dollars in contract value, growing massive product waitlists, and doubling their hiring pipeline.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Quantifies marketing-as-revenue: a strong launch can directly move pipeline, contracts and recruiting, justifying spend on go-direct capability.
insights.quote: We’ve seen startups gain tens of millions of dollars in contract value, growing massive waitlists for their products, doubling their hiring pipeline
insights.locator: These launches aren’t just about looking good

insights.claim: After working with a16z's team, portfolio companies show enormous demand to hire newly-named roles like 'data storytellers,' 'heads of socials,' and 'technical long-form writers,' and a16z is scaling up help for CEOs to build internal New Media capabilities.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: A new category of marketing roles is emerging inside startups, a hiring trend worth tracking for talent platforms and labor-market signals.
insights.quote: we’ve seen enormous demand to hire “data storytellers”, “heads of socials”, and “technical long-form writers” after working with our team.
insights.locator: Once founders see this impact

insights.claim: New Media works not only for already-resonant founders like Brian Armstrong or Palmer Luckey but also for founders with important work who need help fighting the attention battle, because being compelling on a timeline is a distinct, hard-to-cultivate skill.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Suggests the platform's value is broadly distributable rather than reserved for charismatic founders, widening the addressable benefit across a portfolio.
insights.quote: being compelling on a timeline is its own hard-to-cultivate skill.
insights.locator: It’s tempting to read all of this

insights.claim: New Media as practiced is a type of packaging, not content, deploying formats like the 'longform conversation/essay + short-form clips' barbell, guerilla-style amplification such as mastering the QT (quote-tweet) format for algorithmic traction, and press campaigns that land because you don't need them.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: A concrete, repeatable distribution playbook (barbell + clips + QT amplification) that operators can copy to break longform ideas through to niche audiences.
insights.quote: the “one hour podcast, one minute clip” format has proven effective at bringing out what’s interesting about people with something to say
insights.locator: In this sense, “New Media” as we practice it

insights.claim: The goal is not one great launch day but brands that compound over time; founders can learn to make great content by being interesting, while a16z's distinctive teachable skill is how to consistently package it so the brand compounds for the long haul.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Reframes brand as a compounding asset like capital; consistent packaging cadence, not one-off virality, is what builds durable advantage.
insights.quote: where we teach them a useful skill is how to consistently package it, so that their brand will compound and strengthen for the long haul.
insights.locator: This is why having a portfolio services team

themes.theme: Network, talent dataset and AI leverage

insights.claim: a16z's fourth motion leverages a proprietary dataset of 'who is everybody in tech,' meticulously accumulated over years of meetings, now activated with a sprinkle of software and AI to put the network to work for founders, most obviously for hiring.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Decades of relationship data plus AI is being framed as a defensible network-intelligence asset; data accumulation is a long-game VC moat.
insights.quote: We have a pretty great dataset of “who is everybody in tech.”
insights.locator: We have a pretty great dataset

insights.claim: a16z runs talent fellowships (starting with the New Media Fellowship) that gather the best operators in fields like new media, design, growth and forward-deployed engineering, hosting dinners, groupchats and 90+ person multi-day gatherings in Carmel.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Fellowships function as an early-relationship funnel for future founders and a talent-supply channel for the portfolio, deepening the firm's network flywheel.
insights.quote: sometimes invite 90+ people to Carmel for a couple of days to learn from each other and share wisdom.
insights.locator: Fellowships help you meet really high-quality people

insights.claim: The fellowships 'containerize' roles that did not really exist until a few years ago, illustrating the claim that as tools change, jobs change too.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: New tooling (and AI) is spawning entirely new job categories; a leading indicator for labor-market and edtech/recruiting opportunities.
insights.quote: they all containerize roles that never really existed before until a few years ago. As our tools change, our jobs change too.
insights.locator: One thing you might notice about the fellowships

insights.claim: a16z teased upcoming launches of similar communities for executives, emerging investors, and 'something very cool on the help make hiring better for everybody front.'
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Forward signal that a16z is expanding its platform into executive networks and possibly a hiring product; watch for new community/recruiting offerings.
insights.quote: including similar communities for executives, emerging investors, and something very cool on the “help make hiring better for everybody” front
insights.locator: There’s a lot more coming soon

insights.claim: a16z's founding 2009 bet was that a venture firm should be built like a network or like CAA (Firm > Fund) rather than a collection of solo investors, with operating teams constructing platforms that harness network effects, a bet a Harvard Business School study framed and that has decidedly paid off thirteen years later.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Frames New Media as the latest expression of a proven network-effects strategy; the platform model is presented as the structural reason a16z is hard to replicate.
insights.quote: a venture firm should be built like a network (or like CAA) rather than a collection of solo investors (basically Firm > Fund)
insights.locator: When a16z was founded in 2009

insights.claim: Helping founders be interesting constantly over time compounds in value across founder goodwill, portfolio outcomes, follower growth, channel reach, and tacit launch knowledge, but requires both quality and relentless quantity to make a media asset actually compound.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Volume plus consistency, not one-off brilliance, is the compounding mechanism; the cited Elon paraphrase (anyone can make one car) reframes media as a scaled production problem.
insights.quote: To paraphrase Elon, “Anyone can make a nice car once. I’ll be impressed when you can make a million cars.”
insights.locator: A16Z New Media is the newest expression

insights.claim: a16z asserts it has 'one of the most incentive-aligned business models in the world' for helping founders with New Media, because if something is helpful for founders the firm will do it and do it a lot.
insights.speaker: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
insights.so_what: Incentive alignment (equity upside vs agency fees) is the firm's claimed structural edge over fee-based marketing providers; a recruiting and positioning argument competitors must counter.
insights.quote: we have one of the most incentive-aligned business models in the world to help founders with New Media.
insights.locator: Fortunately, we have one of the most

source_meta.author: Erik Torenberg, Alex Danco, Elena Burger, Henry Williams, Tom Hollands, Brent Liang, Gaby Goldberg
